title: AI-First Cost Reduction: How to Drive Sustained, Structural Advantage
gist: Most corporate AI cost-reduction programs underdeliver because companies bolt copilots onto existing workflows instead of redesigning operations around AI; leaders that combine AI-first redesign with traditional cost levers and CEO/CFO ownership achieve roughly 3x the cost reduction of laggards.

insights.claim: Most corporate AI cost-reduction efforts are failing because companies layer copilots and AI tools onto existing workflows, producing theoretical productivity gains that never move actual costs.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: Vendor claims of AI productivity uplift should be discounted; investors should probe whether a company redesigns processes around AI or merely bolts on tools, as only the former reaches the P&L.
insights.quote: Companies are layering copilots and other AI tools onto existing workflows, which generate theoretical productivity improvements but fail to move the needle on costs.
insights.locator: Most AI cost-reduction efforts are failing

insights.claim: In BCG's AI Radar 2026 survey, only 5% of companies reported generating value at scale from AI.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: A 5% success rate signals the AI value gap is real and wide, tempering near-term margin-expansion expectations across most of the corporate universe.
insights.quote: in BCG’s AI Radar 2026 survey, only 5% of companies reported generating value at scale.
insights.locator: Yet success stories like this are rare

insights.claim: Companies fail to capture AI cost savings by falling into five traps: fragmented initiatives with no enterprise path, adding AI on top of processes rather than eliminating them, lack of early proof points, delayed investment awaiting a perfect business case, and targets set on productivity rather than P&L outcomes.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: This is a due-diligence checklist for judging whether a company's AI program is structured to actually cut costs versus generate press-release metrics.
insights.locator: Companies fail to capture AI-driven cost savings

insights.claim: Organizations classified as AI leaders achieve three times greater cost reduction than laggards.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: A 3x dispersion between leaders and laggards implies AI execution will increasingly separate winners from losers within the same sector, a basis for pairs positioning.
insights.quote: organizations that are AI leaders achieve three times greater cost reduction than laggards.
insights.locator: Avoiding these traps requires CEOs and CFOs

insights.claim: Like the steam engine and the spreadsheet, AI delivers only marginal impact when dropped onto pre-existing processes; transformation triggers only when systems are redesigned around the new technology.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: Historical analogy implies the real AI productivity wave lags tool adoption by years, arriving only after organizational redesign, so margin gains should be expected later than hype suggests.
insights.quote: The technology was ready—but the world around it was not.

insights.claim: Cost reduction compounds as enterprises progress from AI-powered workflows to end-to-end redesign around autonomous agents and finally to a new operating system built on multi-agent systems, with labor and external spending declining faster than tech and AI-processing costs rise.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: Signals a spend shift from labor and vendors toward compute and AI infrastructure, favoring AI-processing and agent-platform providers while pressuring headcount-heavy service models.
insights.quote: Labor and external spending decline faster than tech and AI processing costs rise.
insights.locator: This progression has a direct impact on the cost base

insights.claim: A major tech client cut AI-enabled process costs by 50% and achieved 30% annual opex savings against a $15 billion cost base by combining offshoring and contract renegotiation with an 'Eliminate, Simplify, Automate' framework under direct CEO accountability.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the scale of achievable structural savings, but the single-client, vendor-sourced result should be treated as a best case rather than a base rate.
insights.quote: AI-enabled processes saw a 50% cost reduction, and annual opex savings from the $15 billion cost base were 30%.
insights.locator: BCG saw these dynamics at a major tech client

insights.claim: Traditional cost levers like offshoring and vendor renegotiation should fund the AI journey by generating early wins that create financial runway for deeper AI investment.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: Watch for companies self-funding AI transformation from conventional savings rather than raising incremental capex budgets, a sign of disciplined execution.
insights.quote: This shows how AI can amplify traditional cost-cutting to generate early wins.
insights.locator: Fund the journey with traditional levers

insights.claim: Waiting for full ROI certainty costs more than the AI tokens saved, so firms should commit when the business case is clear even if imprecise and enforce hard headcount targets rather than efficiency percentages.
insights.speaker: Jacopo Brunelli
insights.so_what: Hard headcount targets, not productivity percentages, are the credible signal that AI savings will actually reach the P&L; use them as a management-quality tell.
insights.quote: Waiting for full certainty over ROI costs more than the AI tokens you save.
